title: Depression
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Depression
summary: Scripture portrays seasons of deep despair without shame, showing that even the most faithful figures experienced darkness — and that God draws near to the crushed in spirit.
body:
Elijah had just witnessed God's dramatic power on Mount Carmel, yet within days he sat under a broom tree and asked to die [verse:1kgs.19.4]. The prophet was exhausted, isolated, and afraid. God's response was not a rebuke but bread, water, and rest — physical care before spiritual instruction.

The Psalms are unflinching about despair. The poet of Psalm 88 ends his prayer in darkness with no resolution, no tidy conclusion — the most honest psalm in the collection [verse:ps.88.1-18]. And yet this raw cry is still directed toward God, still considered worship. Psalm 34 promises that the Lord is near to the brokenhearted and saves those who are crushed in spirit [verse:ps.34.18].

Depression in Scripture is never treated as evidence of insufficient faith. David, Jeremiah, and Job all experienced profound despair. What Scripture offers is not a formula for instant healing but the steady assurance that darkness does not separate us from God's presence [verse:ps.139.11-12].

Paul describes being pressed beyond measure and despairing of life itself, yet finding that this extremity taught him to rely on God who raises the dead [verse:2cor.1.8-9]. The biblical witness holds sorrow and hope in tension without forcing premature resolution.

verses.annotation: Elijah, depleted after spiritual triumph, asks God to take his life — and God responds with physical care.
The darkest psalm in the Psalter, ending in unanswered darkness, yet still addressed to God.
A direct promise that God is near to the brokenhearted.
Even darkness is not dark to God — night and day are alike to him.
Paul's honest account of despair that became a crucible for deeper reliance on God.
The psalmist preaches to his own downcast soul, choosing to hope in God.
In the midst of devastation, Jeremiah recalls God's mercies that are new every morning.

scholars.quote: Brueggemann contends that the church's reluctance to sit with lament psalms like Psalm 88 has inadvertently communicated that faith requires perpetual cheerfulness — a distortion that leaves suffering believers feeling spiritually deficient.

Goldingay notes that Psalm 42-43 models a pattern of honest self-address — the psalmist interrogates his own despair and redirects himself toward God — a practice that is both emotionally authentic and theologically grounded.

MacArthur reads Elijah's collapse in 1 Kings 19 as a reminder that spiritual leaders are not immune to emotional depletion, and that God's first response to Elijah was physical restoration before theological correction.
